title: The Non-Stochastic Control Problem
speaker: Elad Hazan
abstract: Linear dynamical systems are a continuous subclass of reinforcement learning models that are widely used in robotics, finance, engineering, and meteorology. Classical control, since the work of Kalman, has focused on dynamics with Gaussian i.i.d. noise, quadratic loss functions and, in terms of provably efficient algorithms, known systems and observed state.

proof_stages.label: Linear-dynamical control model and classical baselines
proof_stages.summary: The talk formulates linear dynamical control with state, action, perturbation, and convex cost, then reviews LQR and H-infinity control to identify the gap between stochastic optimality and worst-case robustness.

proof_stages.label: Adversarial policy-regret objective
proof_stages.summary: The non-stochastic control problem is posed with adversarial disturbances and convex costs, measuring performance by policy regret against the best linear feedback controller in hindsight, with the stated goal of an efficient sublinear-regret algorithm under boundedness assumptions.

proof_stages.label: Disturbance-action convex relaxation
proof_stages.summary: Opening the closed-loop recursion shows that optimizing directly over linear feedback matrices is nonconvex, so the controller is reparameterized as a linear function of past perturbations in a larger convex policy class that contains approximations to the linear comparators.

proof_stages.label: Logarithmic memory and slow online optimization
proof_stages.summary: The infinite disturbance history is truncated to logarithmic length while still approximating the best linear policy, and online convex optimization with memory is handled by making gradient updates slowly enough to control the effect of changing policies on future losses.

proof_stages.label: Gradient perturbation controller
proof_stages.summary: Combining the relaxation, truncation, and slow gradient updates yields the gradient perturbation controller, which learns matrices multiplying recent recovered perturbations and is compared empirically with LQR and H-infinity controllers.

proof_stages.label: Moment recovery for unknown dynamics
proof_stages.summary: When the system matrices are unknown, small Gaussian exploratory perturbations allow recovery of Markov parameters such as A^kB, from which the dynamics can be identified sufficiently to run the preceding control method.

proof_stages.label: Spectral-filter basis for partially observed prediction
proof_stages.summary: For partially observed linear dynamical systems, the prediction problem is convexified by learning input-output filters, and a fixed Hankel-matrix eigenbasis approximates geometric impulse responses with few coefficients independently of the condition number.

proof_stages.label: Scope, limitations, and extensions
proof_stages.summary: The speaker summarizes the per-instance robust-control viewpoint, notes limitations such as the symmetric-matrix assumption in the spectral argument, and discusses extensions to slowly time-varying or linearized nonlinear dynamics and to planning applications.
